text: With all due respect YES THE WD HELPED alot really BUT it didn't remove one little speck of glue....it did however put oils into the belt keeping you from testing it to shreds the glue coming off was all you...that's why you had to re apply because the oils has went into the small pores of the belt....that said I have always recommended doing this to get well pretty much anything off something rubbery (no the belt isn't full rubber I know that lol) because it aids in protecting the item you are trying to clean....try it on a hard surface and it would be pointless well may aid in helping you cut off your finger cause you will become grease lightning....cheers...I don't reply back to comments nor look just a heads up to all lol

author: @vtxdaryl
text: I also use WD-40 to remove stickers from automotive parts. If you have an old sticker on your vehicle that you want to remove then just spray the sticker with WD-40, let it sit then get a credit card or plastic scraper and keep applying WD-40 as you are scraping and pulling the sticker off. Then come back with Alcohol to clean off the glue and put some wax over the spot where the sticker was and your are done.

author: @reypolice5231
text:
Just some history on super glue and WD 40.

Super glue was originally created for surgery. It can glue a wound together and thereby not needing stitches.

The body absorbs it, and it pass out of you supposedly.

Super glue dissolves in water. No chemical needed if you keep putting water on it, and time to soak in.

 Water, scrape, let dry, water scrape, let dry, repeat. Yes some soap helps brake the bond.

WD-40

WD-40 started out as ROUND UP Circa 50's for agriculture. They spray crops with it, or did. As there is a huge law suit now for people getting Cancer from it.

What happened was the famers across the pond in the USA started spraying it on there farm equipment and fences. The salesman noticed this and told the company what the farmers were doing with the chemical round up.

That was the start of WD-40.

I don't use it on anything anymore, as I feel it's not healthy for me to get it on my hands or breath it.

author: @Flashahol
text:
WD-40 works even better on stickers; .
Paper-based stickers: spray generously and let soak through. Usually glides right off
Synthetic stickers: use a heat gun to soften the glue and peel the sticker. Most of the glue normally remains, that's where DW-40 shines; you can get the glue off any plastic I've tried and not damage the surface.

text: Excellent video, many thanks. Another useful tip is to remove oil and grease from your hands use ordinary table salt and cooking oil! I use cheap sunflower oil. After rubbing the salt and oil into your hands for several minutes it's necessary to finish off with soap and water. The added bonus of this method is your hands are very soft afterwards.

author: @intelligenttinkering
text:
This is interesting!
It took them 40 attempts to get their water displacing formula to work, but on the 40th attempt, they got it right in a big way. WD-40 Multi Use Product was born. WD-40 stands for Water Displacement, 40th formula
